actions.thought: I am on the button with 8s 7s, which is a very strong speculative hand in a 3-handed game. With 100BB stacks and acting first, the standard play is to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. I will raise to 3x the big blind to 300.

Holding 86o in the Small Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button is an easy fold. The hand has very little playability out of position, and the pot odds do not justify calling with such weak, uncoordinated offsuit cards.

With 7h 5h in the big blind, I am getting roughly 2.25:1 odds to call a 300 raise. Given we are in the early stages of a 150-hand tournament with 100BB stacks, calling is the most balanced approach to realize equity with suited connectors. Folding is too conservative, and raising out of position with this hand is not optimal.

I have bottom pair with 7h 5h on a Qd 7c 4s board. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and allow the aggressor to potentially c-bet, while I evaluate future streets based on their aggression.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a relatively dry Queen-high board. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the opponent checked, I have the option to continuation bet. However, middle pair is a classic 'pot-control' hand. Checking back allows me to keep the pot small, protect my checking range, and potentially induce a bluff or get value on later streets from hands I currently beat. It also avoids getting check-raised off my equity.

I have a pair of 7s and an open-ended straight draw (needing a 3 or an 8 to complete). Since the opponent checked back the flop, their range is potentially capped. Checking is the optimal play here to maintain pot control and realize my equity. If I hit my straight on the river, I will have a strong hand; if I miss, I can re-evaluate my opponent's action. Betting as a semi-bluff might not generate enough fold equity against a player who has already shown hesitation by checking the flop.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a board of Qd 7c 4s 6h. I checked back on the flop to control the pot and keep my hand's strength disguised. Flashlight has checked for a second time on the turn. While I might have the best hand, betting now could lead to a check-raise that forces me to fold my equity, or I might only be called by better hands like a Queen. By checking back again, I am exercising pot control and looking to reach a cheap showdown or catch a bluff on the river.

The board is Qd 7c 4s 6h 5s. I hold 7h 5h for two pair (sevens and fives). The opponent, Flash, has checked both the flop and the turn, which indicates a likely weak or capped range. While I have two pair, there is straight potential on the board (any 8 or 3 makes a straight). Betting here might fold out worse hands and only get called by better ones. Checking is the optimal play to control the pot and get to showdown with my two pair.

On the river, the board completes a straight for me (4-5-6-7-8). Since the opponent has checked every street, including the river, I am almost certainly holding the best hand. I will bet for value, hoping to get called by a Queen or a smaller pair that doesn't believe I have the straight after checking the flop and turn. A bet of 400 into a 650 pot is a standard value sizing in this spot.

The board is Qd 7c 4s 6h 5s, and I hold 7h 5h, giving me two pair (sevens and fives). The opponent, Flash, checked both the flop and the turn, which capped their range. Their sudden 400-chip bet on the river could be a value bet with a Queen or a bluff after missing a draw. While a straight is possible with an 8 or a 3, the passive play leading up to this point makes it unlikely that they slow-played a straight on both earlier streets. My two pair is strong enough to call here, as it beats a single Queen or any missed draw, and the pot odds are favorable (calling 400 to win a pot of 1450). Raising would likely only be called by better hands, so calling is the most balanced and profitable action.

Ten-four offsuit is a very weak starting hand with low equity and poor playability post-flop. Being first to act on the button in a 3-handed game, the most profitable decision is to fold and wait for a better hand to commit chips.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Nine offsuit, heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. This is a favorable position to play aggressively. Raising to 300 allows me to put pressure on the big blind, take control of the initiative, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If called, I have a decent hand to play in position post-flop.

In a blind-versus-blind scenario, J5s is a hand that should be defended against a small blind raise. I am getting 2.5:1 pot odds (200 to call into a 500 total pot) and I will have post-flop position over Flashlight. While the hand isn't strong enough for a standard 3-bet for value, it has enough equity and playability to warrant a call.

I hit top pair (9s) on a dry board (9-4-2). As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a prime spot for a continuation bet. Betting allows me to extract value from lower pairs or hands that might be floating, while simultaneously protecting my hand from potential overcards that could improve on later streets.

I have Jack-high on a dry 9-4-2 rainbow board with no direct draws and very limited backdoor potential. Although Flashlight is offering 5:1 pot odds with a 1/3 pot continuation bet, J5h is at the very bottom of my range and has minimal equity against a typical small blind opening range. Floating here would likely lead to difficult decisions on the turn without a clear path to winning the hand, so I will fold and preserve my stack.

47 offsuit is a very weak, disconnected hand with extremely low equity. Even though I am on the button, there is no strategic justification for investing chips into the pot with this holding. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While I could attempt a steal or limp in, 83 offsuit has very little post-flop playability and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. In a long tournament format, it is better to fold such marginal hands and wait for better spots rather than risking chips with high-disparity cards.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Ace-Deuce offsuit. This is a standard opening hand to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early. Given the stack sizes and the 3-handed dynamics, I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 chips.

We have pocket fives in the Small Blind facing a Button open to 3 BBs. With deep stacks of around 100 BBs, we have excellent implied odds to set-mine. While playing out of position post-flop without a set can be difficult, calling here gives us a chance to hit a strong hand and potentially win a large pot. If we miss the flop, we can easily check-fold.

I am in the Big Blind with A6o. The pot is 700 and it costs 200 to call, giving me favorable pot odds to defend my blind against a raise and a call. Given that I am closing the action pre-flop and have a decent Ace-high hand, calling is the most standard and mathematically correct play to see a flop.

We missed our set on a board with a King, a Nine, and a flush draw. With three players in the pot and being out of position, our pocket fives have very little value. The standard play is to check with the intention of folding if anyone bets.

I hold the nut flush draw (Ad) on a Kd-9c-4d board. Leading out into two opponents, including the pre-flop aggressor, would be overplaying my hand. Checking is the standard play here, allowing me to see the action from the rest of the table and potentially taking a free card or realizing my equity by calling a reasonable bet if the odds justify it.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a King-high board (Kd-9c-4d). Both opponents in the blinds have checked to me. While I only have Ace-high, this texture is generally favorable for the button's opening range. A small continuation bet of 300 (1/3 of the pot) serves to put pressure on the blinds, potentially folding out hands that missed or weak draws, while allowing me to fold cheaply if one of them shows strength.

We missed our set on a board containing a King and a Nine, as well as a flush draw. With the pre-flop raiser continuing with a bet into two opponents, our pocket fives are very unlikely to be the best hand. Continuing out of position without a set or a strong draw would be a losing play. The clear decision is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With a pot of 1200 and a bet of 300, I am being offered 4:1 pot odds to call, which is highly favorable. My hand contains the nut flush draw (Ad) and an overcard, providing significant equity against almost any pair. Calling is the standard, high-value play here, as it allows me to realize my equity and continue the hand without overcommitting or inflating the pot while I am drawing.

I am out of position on the turn with the nut flush draw. Checking is the most prudent strategic choice here, as it allows me to control the pot size and effectively realize my equity. By checking, I can either call a bet if the price is right or take a free card if my opponent checks behind, maximizing my chances of hitting the flush on the river while minimizing risk.

The turn 2d pairs my bottom card, giving me a small pair (2s), but it also completes the diamond flush draw. Flashlight has checked for a second time after calling my flop continuation bet. While I have improved slightly, my hand is still very weak relative to a range that calls a flop bet on this texture. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my current showdown value, and potentially see a cheap river to either improve further or catch Flashlight bluffing with missed draws.

I have flopped the nut flush with the Ace of Diamonds. On a board with three diamonds, my hand is the absolute best possible outcome. Given that my opponent checked behind on the turn, I have a prime opportunity to extract value on the river. I will bet 750 into the 1500 pot to induce a call from any weaker hands such as smaller flushes or top pairs, maximizing the value of my current position.

On the river, the board is Kd-9c-4d-2d-Th. I have a pair of deuces with an Ace kicker. Flashlight has led out for 750 chips into a 1500 chip pot after the turn went check-check. Flashlight's range for calling a flop continuation bet includes many Kings, nines, and diamond draws. While the turn check-check might have induced a bluff, the Ten on the river completes a straight for QJ and improves hands like JT or QT which might have called the flop. My bottom pair is a very weak bluff-catcher here, and there are many hands in Flashlight's range that beat me even if he is thin value-betting. I do not have enough showdown value to justify a call against a 50% pot lead on this texture.
I am dealt 6-4 offsuit on the button, which is a very weak hand with low equity and poor playability. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded. With Ten-Eight offsuit, completing the small blind is a standard play in blind-versus-blind situations to see a flop cheaply. I have already committed 50 chips to the pot, so calling 50 more to match the big blind's 100 is appropriate given the pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with J7o. The Small Blind has limped in, and the Button folded. J7o is a weak hand, but since the Small Blind just called, I have the option to check and see the flop for free. There is no reason to raise here as J7o doesn't play particularly well against a range that might call a raise, and checking allows me to realize my equity without risk.

The flop of 3c-2d-7h is extremely dry and completely missed my hand. With Ten-high, I have no showdown value and no compelling reason to bluff at this pot against the Big Blind's range, which likely includes many small pairs or connectors that hit this board well. I will check to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a very dry board (3-2-7). Since the Small Blind limped pre-flop and checked the flop, I likely have the best hand. I will bet 100 (half pot) to extract value from weaker hands like smaller pairs (3s, 2s, 44-66) or high-card hands that might call one street.

The board texture is extremely low and dry, and my Ten-high hand has completely missed. The opponent has taken the initiative by betting 100 into a 200-chip pot. With no pair, no draw, and virtually no equity to improve to a winning hand, calling is mathematically incorrect. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am in the button position with 7d 8s, which is a marginal hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots. Given that this is a 3-handed game and I want to play a disciplined, tight-aggressive strategy, I will fold rather than invest chips into a potentially weak hand. Conserving my stack is the priority here.

Holding JTo in the Small Blind after the Button has folded, I have a solid hand for a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 (a 3x open) to exert pressure, represent strength, and potentially take the pot down immediately or build value against a wider range of defending hands from the Big Blind.
I hold 8h 4s offsuit in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. This is a very weak hand with extremely low equity and playability, making it an easy fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Eight of diamonds, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Standard strategy dictates opening for a raise here to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. A 3x big blind raise (300 chips) is consistent with my previous betting patterns and should be effective in either winning the blinds immediately or playing a pot in position with a hand that has good post-flop potential.

Ac Qc is a premium starting hand. Facing a raise from the Button, 3-betting from the Small Blind is the standard aggressive play. This builds the pot with a strong hand, aims to isolate the Button, and puts pressure on the Big Blind to fold. Sizing to around 1000 represents a 3x-plus raise, which is ideal from out of position.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind. Pro (Small Blind) has 3-bet, which represents a strong range, and Flash (Button) also needs to act. Folding is too weak given the strength of AQo, but 4-betting is high variance and could isolate me against a range that dominates mine (like AK or KK). Calling is the most balanced play, keeping the pot manageable while maintaining the potential to hit top pair or better on the flop.

I opened for 300 and faced a 3-bet to 1000 from the Small Blind, which was then cold-called by the Big Blind. While J8s is a marginal hand against a 3-bet, I am getting very favorable pot odds (700 to call into a pot of 2300) and I will have the absolute best position for the remainder of the hand. The presence of a third player increases the value of a suited hand like this due to implied odds. I will call to see the flop.

With Top Pair and a strong Queen kicker on a wet A-K-4 flop with two spades, I have a solid but vulnerable hand in a 3-way 3-bet pot. My opponents' ranges are strong, potentially including AK, sets, flush draws, or worse Aces like AJ/AT. Betting around 1/3 of the pot (1000) allows me to extract value from draws, straight draws, and worse pairs while keeping the pot manageable if I encounter heavy resistance.

I have top pair with a Queen kicker on a dry, dangerous board. Pro's bet is 1000 into a 4000 pot, giving me favorable pot odds to call. Raising is too aggressive in a multi-way pot against two opponents, as it could isolate me against a better hand like AK or a set. Calling allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn, while remaining profitable against a wide range of hands that Pro might be continuation betting with.

The flop of Ah-Ks-4h is extremely unfavorable for my hand (Jd-8d). I have no pair and no real draw. Pro, who 3-bet pre-flop, led out for 1000, and Flashlight called. Both opponents are showing significant strength on a board that hits a 3-betting range very hard. Even with 5:1 pot odds, I have almost zero equity to improve to a winning hand and would likely face further bets on the turn. Folding is the only sensible option.

On the turn, the 7d is mostly a brick. We still hold top pair with a strong kicker, but Flashlight's cold-call of a 3-bet preflop and call on an A-K-4 flop indicates a very tight, strong range that could include hands that beat us like AK, or sets, as well as flush draws or worse Aces (AJ, AT). By checking, I control the pot size out of position and can evaluate Flashlight's action. This allows me to bluff-catch against spade draws or potentially reach showdown cheaply without bloating the pot against superior hands.

Pro checked to me on the turn after betting the flop. My Top Pair with a Queen kicker is likely the best hand, but I must remain cautious of his 3-bet pre-flop range which could contain AK, AA, or KK. However, Pro could also be checking to trap, or checking because he has a weaker Ace or a mid-pocket pair that he's given up on. Betting 2000 into the 5000 pot allows me to extract value from weaker Ax hands or second-pair holdings while maintaining control, and it's a size that commits me but allows for a fold if I face a significant check-raise, though I expect to be called by weaker Aces.

By checking the turn, I under-represented my hand to pot control and potentially induce bluffs from missed draws like spade flush draws, or straight draws like QJ/JT. Flashlight's bet of 2000 into a 5000 pot is about 40%, giving me very favorable odds (needing roughly 22% equity) to call with top pair and a strong kicker. The 7d is essentially a blank. Folding here would be overly tight since we still beat many semi-bluffs and occasionally overvalued worse Aces (AJ, AT). Raising would be a mistake, as it would force out bluffs and only get called by hands that crush us, such as AK or sets. Calling is the standard line to keep their range wide and evaluate the river.

The river 9h is a relative blank, though it does complete a JT straight draw. All spade flush draws missed. Since I check-called the turn, leading out (donk betting) the river doesn't make much sense. If I bet, I will fold out all of Flashlight's missed draws and only get called or raised by better hands like AK, two pairs, or sets. By checking, I allow Flashlight the opportunity to bluff with their missed spade draws or straight draws. I can then evaluate their bet size to decide if calling is profitable.

The river is a blank card, and Pro has checked to me again. I hold Top Pair (Ace-Queen), which has significant showdown value. Pro's calling range on the turn likely consists of weaker Aces or King-high hands. While I could potentially value bet, betting thin could be risky if Pro has been trapping with a set or a stronger hand. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and take the pot at showdown against any missed draws or inferior Ace-high hands that he is checking to give up with. This is the safest and most consistent play to secure the current pot without risking further chips against a potential trap.

With T4o on the Button, this hand is far too weak to consider opening, even in a 3-handed game. It lacks playability, high card value, suitedness, and connectedness. The most profitable long-term decision is to fold.

With King-High suited (Kh8h) and the button folding, I am in a prime position to take control against the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that allows me to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot with the initiative, which is a stronger strategic approach than simply completing in the small blind.

I am holding 3h 5d in the Big Blind. The Small Blind has raised to 300. Although it is a blind-vs-blind situation, 53o is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Even with 3:1 pot odds, defending this hand is -EV in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. This is a reasonable hand to open in a 3-handed game to steal the blinds or play in position with the initiative. A 3x raise is a standard size to put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with K9o. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 3x the big blind. While K9o has some high-card value, it is a weak hand to play out of position against an active button open. Calling often invites the Big Blind (Pro) to squeeze or call, and K9o does not play well post-flop in multi-way pots when out of position. To preserve my stack and wait for better spots, folding is the most disciplined play here.

We are in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. While we are getting decent pot odds, 3s 5s is simply too weak to defend out of position. It frequently makes weak draws or dominated pairs. The safest and most standard play is to fold and look for a better spot to invest chips.

I am on the button with 4s 5s, which are suited connectors. In a three-handed game, this is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. My standard opening size throughout the tournament has been 300, and I will maintain that consistency here.

K8o is a weak offsuit hand, and I am out of position in the small blind facing a 3bb open from the Button. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position.

I am in the big blind with Ah-4h. Facing a standard open from the button, calling with a suited Ace is a strong, profitable move. A4s has decent equity and potential to flop top pair, a flush draw, or two pair. Three-betting is an option, but with the current stack depths and position, flatting allows me to realize my equity and play a controlled pot. The price is correct to call.

I have flopped top pair on a very dry, paired board. Given that I am playing out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the most standard and balanced play. I want to keep the pot controlled with a weak kicker and see if my opponent chooses to c-bet, which would allow me to gather more information about their hand strength without bloating the pot unnecessarily. A bet here would likely only get called by hands that beat me or cause the opponent to fold their bluffs.

On a board of 3c-Ad-3s, I am the pre-flop aggressor. This board is very dry and typically favors the range of the pre-flop raiser, as I have more Ace-X hands than the Big Blind. Although I only have five-high, a continuation bet here is highly effective for taking down the pot immediately since Flashlight checked. Most of Flashlight's range (small/medium pairs or random connectors) will find it difficult to continue against a bet on an Ace-high, paired board. I will bet around half the pot to maintain pressure.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a paired, dry board (A-3-3). While my kicker (4) is weak, my hand is currently a strong bluff-catcher against the button's range, which includes many unpaired high cards and smaller pairs. Raising would be suboptimal because it would isolate me against better Aces and force folds from the bluffs I want to keep in the pot. Calling is the correct standard play to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

The board is paired with 3s and an Ace. My pair of Aces with a 4 kicker is a solid hand but vulnerable to better kickers or pocket pairs that might have made trips. Out of position, checking is the most appropriate action to control the pot size. I want to see if my opponent, who bet on the flop, continues to show aggression or checks back, allowing me to take a cheap showdown or induce bluffs.

On the turn, I have improved to a pair of fives, giving me some showdown value. Flashlight's call on the flop (Ad 3c 3s) suggests they likely have a piece of the board, potentially an Ace or a pocket pair. By checking back, I am employing pot control. My hand is not strong enough to value bet, as I am unlikely to get called by worse, but it is strong enough to try to reach a cheap showdown. Checking also allows me to see what Flashlight does on the river.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board of A-3-3-5-Q. My opponent checked the turn after calling the flop, which indicates they likely do not have a strong Ace or a set of 3s. My hand has significant showdown value, but it is not strong enough to value bet effectively, as the opponent would likely fold their bluffs and only call with hands that beat me. Checking is the most balanced and safe play to reach a showdown and realize my equity.

On the river Queen of Hearts, the board is Ace-Three-Three-Five-Queen. Flashlight has checked for the third time (after check-calling the flop and checking the turn). My pair of fives has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value, as I am unlikely to be called by a worse hand. Conversely, it is unlikely I can represent a strong enough hand to force a better hand like a weak Ace or a Queen to fold. Checking back allows me to see the showdown and potentially win the pot with my pair of fives against missed floats or lower pocket pairs.

7s 5c is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Playing it from the button 3-handed is generally unprofitable, especially without strong reads that the blinds over-fold. The standard and most EV+ play here is to simply fold.

With Jack-Five offsuit in the small blind, my hand is too weak to profitably continue, even with the discount provided by the posted small blind. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am holding 9-3 offsuit on the button, which is an extremely weak hand with very little potential equity. In a three-handed game, this hand does not justify an open-raise or any investment. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision to conserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o) after the Button has folded. In a blind-versus-blind situation, A7o is a strong hand that is well ahead of the Big Blind's random range. Raising is the standard play here to either take down the blinds immediately or build a pot with a range advantage. Following my previous sizing patterns, a raise to 300 is appropriate.

Holding [2d, 5d] suited in the Big Blind against a 3x open from the Small Blind. This hand is extremely weak, disconnected, and has low equity even against a wide Small Blind raising range. Calling here would be bleeding chips long-term, and 3-betting as a bluff with such a weak hand is unnecessarily risky. The standard and optimal play is to fold and preserve my stack.

In a 3-handed game, King-six offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button. My previous opens have consistently been to 300, and I will maintain that sizing here to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot pre-flop. K6o has enough equity and raw strength to be profitable as an open in this position.
With 4h 7s in the small blind facing a button raise, this hand is extremely weak and offers no playable value. Folding is the only reasonable decision.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the button. I hold 7-6 offsuit, which is a marginal hand but gets a decent price to call (200 to win 450). Defending the big blind is standard in this spot to avoid being exploited by wide button opening ranges. I will call and look to see a favorable flop or fold if I miss completely.

I have middle pair with 7s, but my kicker is weak and the board is somewhat connected with potential straight and flush draws. As the player out of position in the big blind, checking is the standard play to pot control and see how the button (the aggressor) proceeds. This allows me to re-evaluate based on their action.

The flop of 7h-9c-Jh is very coordinated and likely hits the Big Blind's calling range quite well. My King-six offsuit has some showdown value but doesn't have a strong draw or a made hand on this texture. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, checking back here is more prudent than a continuation bet because the board is likely to result in a call from many of the opponent's hands, and I don't want to get blown off the hand or waste chips on a bluff that has low fold equity. Checking allows me to control the pot and see a turn card for free.

The board texture is extremely coordinated with 7-9-J-T, making it very dangerous. Any 8, Q, or high card could have me beat or be drawing strongly. My pair of 7s is very weak in this context, and leading into the opponent who checked the flop would likely isolate me against better hands while folding out worse ones. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and evaluate the river.

The board is now 7h-9c-Jh-Td, which is extremely coordinated. Any 8 makes a straight, and many other combinations like Qx or two-pair are very possible for the Big Blind. My King-high has very little equity and almost no showdown value, but betting into this texture is dangerous as it heavily favors the defender's range. Since Flashlight checked again, I will check back to see a free river and avoid being check-raised off the hand.

I hold a pair of sevens on a very coordinated board (7-9-J-T-4) where an 8 makes a straight. Since both players checked the flop and the turn, the pot is relatively small and the button (Flash) has likely checked back to control the pot with a marginal hand or air. Betting here is unlikely to generate value, as I will only be called by better hands, and checking allows me to realize showdown value against potential bluffs or missed draws.

The board 7-9-J-T-4 is extremely coordinated, with many possible straights (any 8 or Q). I have King-high with no pair and no straight. While Flashlight has checked three times, indicating weakness, this specific board texture hits a Big Blind's defending range very well. Almost any hand Flashlight called with pre-flop has either a pair or a straight draw that likely arrived. Since I checked the flop and turn, my hand looks weak, and a river bluff is unlikely to fold out even a marginal pair on such a wet board. I will check back and concede the pot if I am beaten, rather than risking chips on a low-equity bluff.

Playing 3-handed, the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds. King-Three suited is a marginal but entirely viable hand to open-raise with, as it has good playability post-flop with flush potential and high card value. A standard 3x raise to 300 chips applies pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind, frequently winning the pot uncontested or giving me the initiative going to the flop.

Ace-Seven suited is a strong hand that plays well in multi-way pots and has good post-flop potential. Calling from the small blind is a standard approach here to realize equity while keeping the pot manageable, as 3-betting out of position can lead to difficult spots against a button opener.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c-6s. The Button (Pro) raised to 3x, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds (200 to call into a 700 pot), but 86o is a very poor hand for a multi-way pot. Being out of position against two players makes it difficult to realize equity unless I hit the board extremely hard. Given the poor playability of this hand, I will fold and preserve my stack.

The flop of Ace-Ace-Six is extremely dry and highly favorable, giving me trip Aces. Being out of position against the button, checking is the optimal strategy to induce a bet from the Pro. Betting now might cause them to fold everything except a better Ace, so I will check to keep their bluffs in the pot or allow them to bet with a smaller pair.

The board is paired with Aces, which heavily favors my pre-flop raising range. Since Flashlight checked, a small continuation bet is a standard play here to apply pressure. A bet of around 30% of the pot (200 chips) is highly effective on this dry texture; it can fold out small pocket pairs, floats, and better King-highs without risking too many chips. If I face a raise, I can easily fold my hand.

I hold trip Aces (Ad 7d) on an Ac 6h As board. This is a very strong hand, and the opponent's small bet of 200 into a 900 pot suggests they may be probing with a weak hand or attempting a small continuation bet. I should not just call; I need to raise to start building the pot and extracting value, while also forcing the opponent to commit more chips if they are holding a weaker Ace or a smaller pocket pair. Raising to 600 (an increase of 400 over their 200 bet) is a standard sizing to accomplish this.

I hold King-high with no strong draws on a paired Ace board. 4h 8c is a weak starting hand with very little potential to improve or win a showdown. As the first to act on the button, there is no need to commit chips with such poor cards. I will fold to preserve my chip lead and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

Flash raised to 3bb from the Small Blind. While I will have position post-flop, K3o is a very weak and easily dominated hand. The price to call is 200, but with K3o, our equity and playability are poor against a standard SB opening range. Folding is the mathematically sound decision here to preserve my stack for better spots.
Pocket pairs are strong in 3-handed play. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size from the button, maintaining consistency with my previous opening ranges and applying pressure on the blinds.
J6 offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Facing a raise from the Button and being out of position, the optimal play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Holding 5s-2s, a very weak hand with low board coverage and poor equity. Being in the big blind against a button raiser puts me out of position for the rest of the hand. Holding 10-7 offsuit on the Button is far too weak of a hand to play, even in position. The standard and optimal play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

With Jack-Nine offsuit in the small blind heads-up against the big blind, completing the blind to see a flop is a standard, low-risk strategy. The hand has decent enough playability, and since I have already posted 50, it only costs 50 more to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has just completed the bet, and the Button (Pro) has folded. I am in the small blind with Jack-Nine offsuit on a flop of 8-10-K. I have an open-ended straight draw (needing a 7 or Queen). Since it is the start of the flop action, checking is a prudent way to control the size of the pot while maintaining the potential to see the turn and river to realize my equity.

I have top pair with a King on a K-T-8 two-spade board. The pot is small (200) since Flashlight just completed the small blind and I checked. Flashlight checked to me on the flop. Betting also protects my hand from various turn cards that could overcard my 3 or complete straights/flushes.

I hold Jack-Nine offsuit on a flop of 8-10-K, giving me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 7 or a Queen). The turn card (2c) is a blank, failing to improve my Jack-Nine offsuit on the 8-10-K board. I still hold an open-ended straight draw (needing a 7 or Queen). I have top pair on a relatively static board. The Deuce of Clubs on the turn is a complete brick and does not change the board texture significantly. Flashlight checked again, which often signifies a medium-strength hand (like a Ten or an Eight) or a draw (spades or a straight draw like QJ). I hold an open-ended straight draw with J-9 on a board of 8-T-K-2. Facing a 200-chip bet into a 500-chip pot, I am getting pot odds of 2.5:1. While my raw equity (8 outs, approximately 17%) is slightly below the required pot odds of 28.5%, the implied odds in this deep-stack game, combined with the potential for me to hit my straight on the river, make calling the high-percentage, positive-expected-value decision.

I missed my straight draw and have no showdown value with Jack-high on a board of 8-T-K-2-3. Since I am first to act on the river, checking is the optimal strategy to avoid wasting chips, as betting would be a bluff with very low probability of success against an opponent who has shown interest in the pot.

The river 3h is an excellent card for me as it improves my hand to two pair (Kings and Treys). I will bet 400 (half the pot) to extract value from any Kx hands that Flashlight is likely to call with, assuming I am still betting a missed draw or a weaker King.

Flash has bet on the river after consistent aggression on the turn. My Jack-high has no showdown value, and even if the opponent is bluffing with missed spade draws, they may still have Ace-high or Queen-high which beats my Jack-high. Calling here with no pair is statistically a losing play, so folding is the prudent decision.

My hand, 6h 2h, is extremely weak and lacks the high-card strength or connectivity to play profitably from the button. There is no incentive to play a speculative hand in this spot, especially with two opponents left to act, so folding is the correct and disciplined decision.

I am in the Small Blind and it has folded to me. My hand, K6o, is strong enough to attempt a steal against the Big Blind. I am in the Big Blind holding 8c 3d, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, this hand lacks the equity and playability post-flop to justify a call or a 3-bet. The optimal and standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with K7o in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. My stack is healthy, and I've been successfully opening for 300 chips in similar situations previously. K7o is well within the opening range for a button in this format.

Pocket deuces is the lowest pair and plays very poorly out of position. Even though Flash seems to be opening frequently from the Button, flatting from the Small Blind carries the risk of getting squeezed by the Big Blind, and we will struggle to realize our equity post-flop unless we hit a set. Given the positional disadvantage and the fact that 22 is easily counterfeited, folding is the most solid and standard play.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the button. My hand, 6h Th, is suited and has reasonable playability. With 200 chips to call to win a total pot of 650, the pot odds are roughly 3:1 (approximately 30.7%). Given my stack size and the position, calling is the standard, disciplined play to see a flop rather than 3-betting with a marginal hand.

I am in the big blind with 10-high, facing a board of 3d 4c 4d. I have no pair, no strong draw, and essentially no equity to value bet or semi-bluff against the pre-flop raiser. Checking is the only logical play to see if the opponent continues or checks back.

I opened pre-flop from the button and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes 3-4-4, which is a very dry board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage here. I am in the big blind with 10-high, facing a continuation bet on a board of 3d 4c 4d. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. There is no strategic justification for calling or raising with such a weak holding against an aggressive opponent. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

T3o is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and low high-card value. Being on the button does not make up for how unplayable this hand is post-flop. The mathematically sound choice is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (5-2 offsuit). While the pot odds to call and complete the blind are tempting, playing a garbage hand out of position against an aggressive opponent like Flash is a losing proposition in the long run. I am on the button with King-high, which is a playable hand for an open in a 3-handed game. Opening to 300 is the standard sizing I have been using throughout the tournament to steal blinds or take initiative in position.

Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the Small Blind. Given that Flashlight raised from the Button, I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position with a range-disadvantaged hand. It's a standard fold.
I hold Q3o in the Big Blind facing a 3bb raise from the Button. This hand is too weak to defend out of position and plays poorly post-flop. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Queen-Eight offsuit. While not a powerhouse, this hand is well within the standard opening range for a button steal. I have been consistently opening for 3x the big blind (300 chips) throughout the tournament, and I will continue that strategy here to maintain pressure and try to take down the blinds.

I am holding 5s 3c in the small blind, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and unconnected hand. Facing a 3x open-raise from the button, I have no justification to call or 3-bet. The most sensible and standard play is to fold and preserve my stack for a much better starting hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s-6d, which is a weak hand with poor connectivity and low equity against a button raise. Calling 200 into a 450 pot with such a weak holding out of position is not mathematically sound. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand.

We are 3-handed and I am first to act on the Button with Jack-Ten offsuit. This is a standard hand to open-raise in short-handed play, as it has good blockers and playability post-flop if called. I'll make a standard raise to 3 big blinds (300) to steal the blinds or play a pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h-9s, which is a weak hand to defend against a Button open. I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit (Ah 9d). The Button (Pro) raised to 300, and the Small Blind folded. I have flopped top pair with an Ace, but the board (Ad Th Kd) is extremely coordinated and hits a button's opening range very well. The board is Ad Th Kd, which heavily connects with the Big Blind's calling range. I have bottom pair and a gutshot straight draw. I have top pair with a nine kicker on a very coordinated board (Ad Th Kd 6s). The board has double-paired Kings alongside an Ace and a Ten. My hand is Aces and Kings with a Ten kicker. Checking behind takes my hand to showdown, where I expect to win against his busted draws.

Holding pocket sevens on the button in a three-handed game is a strong position. In a 3-handed game, Queen-Jack offsuit is a strong hand to defend against a Button open. I have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw on a King-high board in a three-way pot. As the Small Blind, I am first to act on the flop. With pocket eights on a K-9-6 board, I have third pair in a multi-way pot. I am the pre-flop aggressor holding pocket sevens on a K-9-6 rainbow board. Both opponents have checked to me. On a board of K-9-6, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10) and two overcards. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has placed a continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 900. My overcards (Queen and Jack) are unlikely to be clean outs given the King on board and the presence of two opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play here.

With pocket eights on a K-9-6 rainbow board, I only have third pair. Flashlight, the pre-flop raiser, fired a standard continuation bet. Their range easily contains Kings, Nines, or higher pocket pairs. Being out of position with a marginal hand and only two outs to improve to a set, calling or raising here is too speculative and risky. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 5c 3h, which is a very weak hand. In a 3-handed game, while stealing the blinds is important, 53 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and not worth a raise. Folded to me in the small blind. A3o is a decent hand to attempt a steal against the big blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on Flashlight to defend with marginal holdings, and having an Ace blocks some of their strongest possible hands.

Pro raised from the small blind, which is often a wide range. I am in the big blind with King-Jack suited, a strong hand that plays well post-flop. I have top pair (Jacks) on a board of T-J-7. Pro checked to me, which suggests either weakness or a trap. Given the two-tone board with potential draws, I want to charge for cards and get value from weaker hands like a Ten, or a lower pair. I am on the button with 3d 5h (5-3 offsuit). This is a very weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand with almost no potential to make strong hands post-flop. Opening this pre-flop is unprofitable and unnecessary, so the standard and best play is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind with a weak suited hand (Ts 2s). I initially completed the small blind to see a flop cheaply, but the big blind has raised to 300. I have Kc 8c in the Small Blind. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. In a 3-handed game, King-Eight suited is a strong enough hand to continue with. Holding 4d 6h, an offsuit two-gapper, in the big blind facing a button raise and a small blind call. This hand is far too weak to defend, especially out of position against two players. Folding is the standard and mathematically sound decision.

I have top pair (Eights) with a King kicker on a relatively low, draw-heavy board (8s 5s 3d). I am on the button with Queen-high on a board of 8s-5s-3d. The turn card (Ace of spades) significantly impacts the board, potentially completing a flush or providing the opponent with a pair of Aces. My hand (Qd, 7c) has no equity against an Ace or a made flush. Betting now would be a pure bluff into a board that favors the opponent's calling range, and there is a high probability of being check-raised or called. The river completes a four-spade board (8s 5s 3d As 6s). I hold a pair of eights (Kc 8c) but do not have a spade. In this situation, any single spade in the opponent's hand makes a flush. The board has developed into a four-flush board on the river with the 6s. My hand (Qd, 7c) has no spades and lacks any pair, meaning I have no showdown value against any spade or any Ace. I am on the button in a 3-handed game with 84 suited. This is a marginal but playable hand in this position. 4d 9d is a very weak and disconnected hand, even though it's suited. Against a 3x open from the Button, folding from the Small Blind is the standard and safest play to conserve our stack.

QTo is a reasonable hand to defend in the big blind against a button raise. I have flopped the absolute nuts with a broadway straight (9-T-J-Q-K) using my Q-T on the 9-J-K board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Flash), who is historically aggressive, the optimal play is to check. On the button, I raised pre-flop with 8h4h and was called by the Big Blind (Flashlight). If I bet and get raised or called, I'm in a tough spot with zero equity. I'll check back and see if the turn improves my situation or if I can find a better spot to bluff later.

I hold the nut straight (9-10-J-Q-K) on a board of 9-J-K-6. Since Flash checked the flop, I want to induce a bet or keep them in the hand with marginal holdings by checking again. Being out of position makes a check-call or check-raise on the river more effective, rather than betting now and potentially causing them to fold a range that has likely missed or is weak.

On the turn, the 6 of clubs doesn't change the board texture significantly. I currently have 8-high with zero equity and no draws. Flashlight has checked for the second time, which could indicate weakness, but the board (K-J-9-6) is very well-connected and likely hit a large portion of the Big Blind's defending range. Since I checked back on the flop, my range looks capped. While a bluff might occasionally work, the risk of being called by a Jack, a King, or even a medium pair is high. I will check back to see the river for free and avoid potentially spewing chips with no outs.

I have completed a straight (9-10-J-Q-K) with my ten and queen. The board shows 9-J-K-6-7, with three clubs present. Although a flush is possible, the opponent's passive check-check-check line across the flop and turn suggests they likely do not have a flush, as they would have bet a flush draw or completed flush. Given the board texture and the action, I have the effective nuts or a very strong hand. A value bet is necessary to extract chips from any kings, jacks, or pairs the opponent might have held that were too weak to bet earlier.

On the river, the board is 9c Jd Ks 6c 7c. I have 8-high and have checked back on both the flop and the turn, showing no interest in the pot. Flashlight has now led for 400 into a 650 pot. With A-Jo on the button, I have a strong starting hand in the best position. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard play to take the initiative, pressure the blinds, and potentially win the pot pre-flop or build it for post-flop play.

I hold Pocket Kings, a premium hand that deserves immediate aggression. Since I am in the Small Blind and facing a raise from the Button, I need to take control of the hand. Flat-calling would invite the Big Blind in and leave me playing a bloated pot out of position. A 3-bet to 1000 is the correct size to build the pot and define my hand strength, setting me up to dominate the hand or potentially get a call from a weaker Ace or lower pair.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Spades and Seven of Clubs, a weak offsuit hand. The Button (Pro) raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) 3-bet to 1000. Flashlight's 3-bet from the small blind into two players suggests a very strong range (likely JJ+, AQ+). Given my hand strength and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against two active opponents, there is no reason to commit any chips to this pot. Facing a large 3-bet from the Small Blind, AJo is easily dominated by hands in their 3-betting range, such as AK, AQ, and large pocket pairs. Calling would require investing a significant portion of our stack out of position with a hand that often faces reverse implied odds post-flop. I hold a pocket pair (3s 3h) on the button in a 3-handed game with a healthy stack. Being in position, I want to take the initiative. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open that allows me to either pick up the blinds or play a pot in position, where I have the advantage. This also gives me a chance to hit a set on the flop.

I am holding 6d 2h in the small blind. This is a very weak hand with almost no potential to improve or play well out of position. Flashlight, the chip leader, has raised from the button to 300. On a board of Th-Qs-As-Ac, my pocket nines are significantly weakened. There are three overcards, and any Ace, Queen, or Ten currently beats me. The river is the King of Spades, completing a Broadway straight on the board (A-K-Q-J-T) with my Jack. 78 offsuit is generally too weak to open, even from the button in a 3-handed game. Folding is the standard, safest play to preserve my stack for better starting hands.

I have 2h-4h and the board is Tc-Td-Ah-Ac. My hand has no showdown value, and there is no reason to bluff in a pot where I have shown weakness by checking earlier streets. Checking is the most prudent play to minimize any further loss.

The board is now double-paired with Aces and Tens (Ah Ac Tc Td). My hand is currently Two Pair, Aces and Tens, with a Queen kicker (Qd). Since I have significant showdown value with a Queen kicker and there is little to be gained by turning my hand into a bluff or betting for thin value against a range that likely folds worse and calls with better (like a King), checking is the most stable and prudent play to reach the river for free.

The board is paired with Aces and Tens, making it very likely that my opponent has a piece of this board (either a Ten or an Ace), both of which are calling ranges. The board is double-paired with Aces and Tens and has a Queen as the high card (Ac Ah Tc Td Qs). My hand (Qd 4s) does not improve upon the board's five cards, meaning I am 'playing the board.' I am in the button position with 4h-3d, which is a very weak starting hand with little playability. The flop is Kd Kh 5d, giving us Ace-high on a paired board. While this board typically favors the pre-flop aggressor, I am out of position against Pro. Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand and does not meet the requirements for a standard open-raise from the button, even in a three-handed game. Flashlight has shown a willingness to call raises and defend the big blind frequently, so I will opt for a disciplined fold here.
