While I have the pot odds to call, 6-2 offsuit is a bottom-tier hand with very poor playability post-flop, even in position-advantaged or disadvantageous spots. It is mathematically and strategically better to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable situation rather than defending with such a marginal holding.

We're on the Button with Ten-Nine offsuit. While it has some straight potential, T9o is generally too weak to open-raise profitably in this situation, especially with opponents who have shown aggression and given our slightly shorter stack. The most standard and conservative play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

With the Button having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. Jack-Queen offsuit is a strong starting hand in this scenario, and I want to take control of the pot pre-flop. Raising to 300 is a standard play to pressure the Big Blind and potentially win the pot immediately or play a larger pot with an advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit (Ac 9s). Flashlight, who has been quite active, raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given that there are only three players remaining and I have a strong Ace, this is a clear continuing hand. Calling is standard here to see a flop in position against the Small Blind, though a three-bet (raise) is also viable. However, since Flashlight's ranges have been somewhat unpredictable and I have a stack lead, calling keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to utilize my positional advantage post-flop.

The flop of 2h-2c-Ad is extremely dry and heavily favors a calling range that likely contains an Ace. As the pre-flop aggressor out of position, checking is the most strategic play. My hand (Queen-high) has very little showdown value, and betting into a likely stronger range would be inefficient. I will check to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

On this extremely dry, paired board (2-2-A), my Top Pair with a Nine kicker is very strong. Flashlight, the pre-flop raiser, has checked to me. While checking back to induce bluffs or control the pot is a valid strategy, I prefer a small value bet here. A bet of 200 (one-third of the pot) can get called by pocket pairs (33-KK) that don't believe I have the Ace, or potentially by a weaker Ace. It also prevents Flashlight from realizing equity with random overcards for free. If Flashlight folds, I'm happy to take the pot now; if they call, I can re-evaluate on the turn.

The flop of 2-2-A is extremely dry, and I have absolutely no piece of this board with Queen-high. My opponent's bet into me on this board could be a probe bet or a continuation of their range, but calling with Queen-high without any equity or showdown value is not a sound long-term strategy. Folding is the most disciplined and effective move to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine suited (Jd 9d). This is a strong enough hand to open from the button, especially in a three-handed game. Opening to 300 (3BB) allows me to potentially take down the blinds or play the hand in position against either the small or big blind. Given my stack size and position, raising is the most strategically sound approach.

I am in the Small Blind with T3o, a very weak hand. Flashlight, who is on the Button, has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, Button raises are common, but T3o is near the bottom of my range and does not play well out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I've been fairly active, there is no reason to defend such a weak hand against a standard raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We are in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Button. We hold A4o, which is a weak offsuit Ace that plays poorly out of position. While it has some high-card value, calling a 3x raise OOP often leads to difficult post-flop spots, especially when we pair our Ace but face kicker issues. The most prudent and standard play here is to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am on the button with 3s 4s, a small suited connector. My stack is healthy at 11,750 chips. While the hand is speculative, opening from the button is generally profitable to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. However, considering I have been relatively active and 3-4 suited is very bottom-of-range, I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive table image and potentially take the blinds down immediately. If I am 3-bet, I can easily fold.

With Ace-Four suited in the Small Blind facing a Button open, a 3-bet is a strong play. It puts pressure on the Button's wide opening range, denies equity to the Big Blind, and gives me a good chance to take down the pot pre-flop. If called, I have a hand with good playability that can make the nut flush or straight. Sizing to 1000 is consistent with my previous 3-bet sizing from the blinds.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a weak hand to play against a 3-bet from the small blind. The pot odds do not justify calling 900 chips with such a dominated hand, and 4-betting would be overplaying a marginal holding. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

My initial raise with 3s 4s was a speculative attempt to take the pot from the button. Pro's 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1000 indicates a strong range. While I have the benefit of position, 3s 4s is too weak to continue against this level of aggression. Calling would likely lead to difficult post-flop scenarios where I am significantly behind, so folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.
AJo is a strong hand 3-handed and merits an open-raise from the button. I'll make a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative, or pick up the blinds right here.

QJo is a reasonable hand to defend from the Small Blind against a standard button open. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the pot odds are sufficient to warrant a call to see a flop, provided the Big Blind does not squeeze. Keeping the range wide and seeing a flop is the standard strategic play here.

I am in the big blind with 52 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I am getting good pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 900), I will be out of position for the entire hand against two opponents. Given my hand strength and the likelihood of being dominated or missing the flop entirely, I will choose to preserve my chips and fold.

I have flopped top two pair (Queens and Jacks) on a board that is quite coordinated with straight and flush draws. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Pro), the standard and most effective play is to check. This allows the aggressor to potentially fire a continuation bet with air, a draw, or a weaker hand (like an Ace or a Ten), which I can then respond to with a check-raise to build the pot.

On a very wet and coordinated board (Js Th Qs), my As Jh gives me middle pair, an open-ended straight draw, and the nut flush blocker. This board connects heavily with the small blind's calling range, meaning I could be facing hands like two-pair, straights (AK/K9), or strong combo draws. While a bet could deny equity or get value from weaker draws, checking back allows me to pot-control, guarantee I realize my strong equity, and avoid getting blown off my hand by a check-raise. It also disguises my hand, giving me the opportunity to bluff later if a spade hits or extract value if I improve.

The board is highly coordinated with potential straight (K9, 97) and flush draws. Having top two pair (Queens and Jacks), I hold a strong but vulnerable hand. The opponent checked behind on the flop, which suggests they either have a marginal hand (Ace-high, weak pair) or are slow-playing a monster. Given the texture of the turn (8d), I cannot afford to give a free card, as many draws or even a straight could improve on the river. Betting now allows me to extract value from inferior pairs and draws while maintaining the betting lead, protecting my hand from being outdrawn by a free card.

With middle pair, an open-ended straight draw, and the nut flush blocker (As), I have plenty of equity to see the river. Facing a reasonable bet of 400 into 700, my direct odds aren't perfect for just the straight draw, but my combined outs, implied odds when hitting the nut straight, and the potential to bluff spade runouts using the As make this a clear call in position.

On this river, my top two pair (Queens and Jacks) remains a strong hand. The board is somewhat coordinated, but the river 6c did not complete any obvious flush draws, and the straight possibilities (like 9-7) require specific hole cards that the opponent would have had to play from the flop. Given the opponent called my turn bet, they likely have a marginal hand like a Ten, a Jack, or a drawing hand that missed. Checking might lead to the opponent checking behind, whereas betting allows me to extract value from their weaker pairs or second-best hands. A value bet of 600 in a 1400 pot is a solid sizing to get called by a Ten or a weaker Jack while remaining foldable if raised, which would represent a straight or better.

On this river, my pair of Jacks is merely third pair on a highly coordinated board. Flashlight's lead on the turn and continuation on the river represents at least a strong one-pair hand (like a Queen), two pair, or a straight. I also hold the As, which actually blocks the nut flush draw he could potentially be bluffing with. With many better hands in my range to bluff-catch or call with (such as Qx, Kx, or 9x combos), folding this weak third pair is the safest and most standard play.

Holding King-Seven offsuit on the button is a weak hand. Despite being in a late position, there is no value in playing this hand. Folding is the most conservative and strategic decision to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s2s. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro). This is a Blind vs Blind situation. 4s2s is a weak hand, but it is suited. I have several options: fold, call, or raise. Given that Pro's stack is shorter (6750) and I have a healthy stack, I could try to steal the blinds, but a raise here would likely be met with resistance from a competent player, and calling (completing) might just invite a raise from Pro. While 42s has some post-flop potential, it's generally better to play tighter from the SB when the BB is a solid player. However, in a tournament setting with 150 hands and at the halfway point, maintaining a balanced range is important. That said, 42s is at the very bottom of any reasonable raising or calling range. Folding is the most disciplined play to avoid playing out of position with a marginal hand against a short-stack who might be looking for a spot to double up.

I am on the button with 6c 5c, which are solid suited connectors for a 3-handed game. Opening from the button with this hand is standard to apply pressure on the blinds and gain initiative in the pot. Flashlight (BB) has shown a willingness to defend but also folds to consistent post-flop pressure, and Pro (SB) has been playing relatively tight. I will raise to 300, which is my standard opening size, to try and take the blinds or see a flop in position.

AJo is a strong hand that plays better as a 3-bet from the Small Blind against a Button open. By 3-betting, we put pressure on the Button's wide opening range, discourage the Big Blind from overcalling or squeezing, and take the initiative going to the flop. A sizing of 1000 (about 3.3x the open) is standard out of position.

7-4 suited is a weak, speculative hand that is poor to defend with, especially against a 3-bet from the small blind and an initial raise from the button. There is no incentive to call 900 chips with this holding, as it is dominated by most ranges that would 3-bet. Folding is the standard, disciplined play.

Pro has consistently 3-bet to 1000 over my 300 opens in previous hands (Hands #7, #39, and #73), indicating a strong and disciplined 3-betting range from the blinds. While 6c 5c is a decent speculative hand in position, calling 700 more into a 1400 pot against a tight opponent's 3-bet is risky. Given the effective stack size (Pro has 5900 remaining), the implied odds for a low suited connector are marginal against a range that likely includes high pairs and strong broadways. I will fold and preserve my chip lead for better opportunities.

Q7s is a marginal suited gapper. While it can be opened from the button in some situations, it's usually better to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand, especially with two aggressive players in the blinds.

I hold [10c, 2d], which is a very weak hand. Even though I am in the small blind and the pot odds for calling the remaining 50 are decent, the hand itself has very poor playability. Given the stack sizes and the tournament stage, it is not worth investing more chips to contest this pot against the big blind. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision.

With Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a very strong hand. It is standard to open from the button to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds. An open raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous playstyle and provides a good balance between folding out weak hands and building a pot when called.

I am in the small blind with a very weak offsuit hand (7d 4s). Flashlight has raised from the button, and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if I continue. Given the low quality of the hand and poor position, there is no reason to defend. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, A3o is a marginal hand to play out of position. While it has an Ace blocker, its poor playability post-flop makes calling a losing play in the long run. A 3-bet bluff is an option, but with our stack size of 72 BBs, it's safer to fold and wait for better spots or hands with more playability/equity.

I am on the button with 8c 4d. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game. Looking at the table dynamics, both Pro and Flashlight have been active in defending their blinds and re-raising. With over 70 hands remaining and a healthy stack, there is no reason to attempt a steal with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
J6o is a very weak offsuit hand. Being out of position in the small blind against the big blind, the most +EV play is to simply fold and save our chips for a better opportunity.

9d8d is a strong suited connector that plays very well in position. Raising from the button to 3x allows me to either steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that can flop well, making straights or flushes.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a strong enough hand to defend against a button raise. Given my stack size and the current pot odds, calling is the most balanced approach to see a flop in position against the button, keeping the pot manageable while maintaining equity.

I am holding 9-2 offsuit in the Big Blind. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a 700 pot), this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop, especially in a multi-way pot out of position. I have a healthy stack and there's no reason to commit chips with such a low-equity hand.

The flop brings an Ace, which is a card that strongly favors the button's opening range. With Queen-high and no draw, I have effectively missed the board completely. Betting here would be an unnecessary bluff against an opponent likely holding an Ace. I will check to evaluate the opponent's action, but I am prepared to fold to any significant bet, as my hand has virtually no showdown value.

I missed the flop, but the A-high board heavily favors my pre-flop raising range. A continuation bet of around half the pot is standard here to represent top pair and apply pressure, likely folding out the small blind's unpaired hands and smaller pocket pairs. I also have backdoor flush and straight equity if called.

The board shows an Ace, which perfectly hits the button's opening range. With only Queen-high and no pair or draws, I am essentially drawing dead against any Ace or pocket pair. Calling a bet of 300 here would be a mistake, as I have no showdown value and minimal equity to improve. Folding is the only sensible choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am holding a very weak, unsuited hand (3-7 offsuit) with no potential for strong combinations. In a three-handed game, there is no strategic reason to invest chips into the pot with this holding, regardless of position. It is best to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous hand.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket Nines, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, especially when the button has folded. My standard opening raise is 300, and I will continue with that strategy here to either win the pot immediately or build it against the Big Blind (Pro).

K2o is a weak offsuit king with high reverse implied odds. Although I would have position post-flop against the Small Blind, calling a 3x raise with such a marginal hand is unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

I am on the button with 9h 6s. While it is a 3-handed game and I have been opening a wide range, 96o is near the bottom of the range and generally considered a fold even from the button against observant opponents. Pro (SB) has shown a willingness to 3-bet recently, and Flashlight (BB) is the chip leader. I will wait for a better spot to maintain my stack and table image.

With 6h2c in the small blind, we have one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite only needing 50 more chips to call, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is heavily unprofitable. Folding is the only sensible option.

With A7 suited on the Button, this is a strong starting hand to open the action. A standard raise to 3x the Big Blind will build the pot with a hand that has good high card value and flush potential, while also possibly picking up the blinds uncontested.

Jh6h is a weak suited hand. Being in the Small Blind against a Button raise puts me out of position for the rest of the hand, making it difficult to realize equity. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 6c, a strong suited connector that plays very well post-flop. The Button (Pro) has opened to 300, and with the Small Blind folding, I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. This gives me pot odds of roughly 2.25-to-1, which is more than enough to justify a defend with this hand. While I could 3-bet as a bluff, calling allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable against a reasonably sized stack (71 BBs). I will call and see a flop.

On this 9s 3h 4s board, I have 7c 6c, which gives me a backdoor straight draw and two overcards to the 3 and 4. The board is relatively dry, though there is a spade draw. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro), the standard play is to check to the raiser to see their continuation bet. I don't have enough value to lead out, and my hand has some equity to continue if they bet a reasonable amount.

With Ah 7h on a 9s 3h 4s flop, I have missed entirely, holding only Ace high with backdoor flush and straight draws. The board is wet with spades and low cards, which connects fairly well with the Big Blind's pre-flop calling range. A continuation bet might get called or check-raised frequently here, forcing me to fold. Checking back exercises pot control, allows me to realize my equity, and gives me a free look at the turn where I might improve.

The Turn card is an Ace of Clubs. While an Ace is generally better for the pre-flop raiser's range (Pro), the fact that Pro checked back on the 9-3-4 flop suggests they may not have a strong hand or were playing a marginal hand cautiously. With my 7-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5), I have very little showdown value. However, leading into the pre-flop aggressor on an Ace turn is often seen as a 'donk bet' and might not be as effective against a professional-caliber player who understands range advantages. I will check to see how Pro reacts. If Pro checks back again, I get a free look at the river to potentially hit my straight. If Pro bets, I will evaluate the price offered to chase my 4 outs.

The turn brings an Ace, giving me top pair. Flash checked again, indicating weakness or a drawing hand. Flash's range includes marginal pairs like 9x, pocket pairs, and various draws (spades, 5-6, etc.). Because the board is draw-heavy, checking back again would give away free equity. I need to bet for value and protection to charge draws and get value from weaker pairs. A bet of around 60% of the pot accomplishes this.

On this board, I have 7-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Pro checked back the flop but has now bet 400 into a pot of 650 on the Ace turn. While the Ace is a scare card, it also hits a significant portion of a Button opening range. My 4-outer gutshot (8.7% equity) does not provide sufficient direct pot odds to call a 60% pot bet (requiring ~27.5% equity). Furthermore, out of position, it is difficult to realize any implied odds if I do hit on the river. Bluffing with a check-raise is risky against a player who may have checked the flop with a marginal Ace or a draw that has now improved. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding Ace-Four suited on the button is a strong position to open the action. I have a significant positional advantage and a hand with decent equity, so raising to 300 is a standard move to either steal the blinds or take the initiative into the flop.

Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button. Queen-Seven suited is a marginal hand to defend from the Small Blind against a 3x open. Playing out of position against a deep-stacked opponent with a hand that is easily dominated or difficult to play on many textures is generally unprofitable. Furthermore, the Big Blind still has the opportunity to squeeze. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, A5o is a marginal offsuit hand to play from the Big Blind. Being out of position with a weak kicker makes it vulnerable to domination (reverse implied odds) if an Ace hits the board. While it could be used as a light 3-bet bluff occasionally, folding is a solid and low-variance play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with King-Trey suited (Ks 3s). In a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand from the button to put pressure on the blinds and take advantage of positional play. I have been consistently opening for 3x (300 chips) throughout the tournament, and I will maintain that sizing here to keep my range balanced and potentially win the blinds or play a pot in position.
I am in the Small Blind with an offsuit gapper, 8h 6c. Facing a raise from the Button, this hand is far too weak to call or 3-bet from out of position. The correct play is to fold.

I am in the big blind with Jack-high, specifically Jh-3d. This is a weak, disconnected hand, and despite getting decent pot odds (200 to call for a 450 pot), it is very difficult to play out of position against a button raiser like Flash. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to conserve chips and wait for a better opportunity.
With Q7o on the button, my hand is too weak to open-raise or call. The best and most standard action is to fold and wait for better starting hands.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded, meaning I am heads-up against the big blind. King-Jack offsuit is a strong hand in a three-handed game, and I have the positional advantage post-flop. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 5s, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight, the current chip leader, has raised from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, this hand plays poorly post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. There is no need to defend with such a low-equity hand at this stage of the tournament.

With Ace-Six offsuit on the button, I have a clear opportunity to open-raise and take control of the hand. My stack size is healthy, and this is a standard open from the button position to steal the blinds or play in position.

I am in the Small Blind holding 6h 9d. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. While the price to call is relatively small (250 more), 96o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an active button and a strong player in the Big Blind. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand given my stack size and the poor hand quality.

I hold J9 suited in the Big Blind facing a standard button open from Flashlight. J9s plays very well post-flop and has good playability against a wide button opening range. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 450), this is a profitable flat call to see a flop.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with a 9 kicker on a moderately wet board (Qd Jc 4d). Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the standard play. It allows me to control the size of the pot, keep the opponent's bluffing range wide, and evaluate their continuation bet sizing before committing more chips.

I raised pre-flop and am the aggressor on this board of Jack-Queen-Four with two diamonds. Even though I only have Ace-high, a continuation bet of about half the pot is a standard play here to represent a strong hand, especially since the opponent just checked. I have an overcard to the board, which provides some potential for improvement if called.

I have middle pair with a 9 kicker on a Q-J-4 board with a flush draw present. The opponent's continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 650 is standard and gives me favorable pot odds to call. My J9s is definitely strong enough to defend against a button c-bet range, which includes many draws, worse pairs, and air. Raising would only fold out their bluffs and keep them in with better hands like Qx or better Jx, so calling is the optimal play here to evaluate the turn.

The turn card is an Ace, which is a terrible card for my hand as it hits the pre-flop raiser's range extremely hard. My middle pair of Jacks is now a third pair, and many of the opponent's overcards or draws have improved. Checking here to exercise pot control and see the opponent's action is the only viable option. I plan to fold if faced with another significant bet.

I have improved to a pair of Aces on this turn. The board is somewhat draw-heavy, with two diamonds and potential straight draws. By betting now, I can extract value from weaker hands like a single Jack or Queen, while also protecting against potential draws. A bet of approximately half the pot is standard to balance value and protection.

The Ace on the turn is a terrible card for my hand, demoting my middle pair to third pair and hitting the opponent's pre-flop raising and flop c-betting ranges extremely hard (e.g., AK, AQ, AJ, AT, KT). I have no strong draws to improve on the river. As planned on the flop, facing a significant turn barrel on this board texture, folding is the safest and most optimal play to preserve my stack.

I am on the Button with Ace-five offsuit. In 3-handed play, Ace-high hands are generally strong enough to open from the Button to steal the blinds or play a pot with positional advantage. I will raise to my standard 3x (300) to keep my opening range consistent and exert pressure on the blinds.

I hold 9To in the Small Blind facing a 3bb open from the Button. This is a weak offsuit connector that plays poorly out of position, and there is still the Big Blind left to act behind me. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Flash is opening a wide range from the button, and I am in the big blind with a suited hand. The pot odds are favorable (200 to call for a potential total pot of 650), making a call a standard defensive play to see a flop.

I have a flush draw with the Jack of Diamonds on an Ace-high, Queen-high, diamond-heavy board. As the out-of-position player in the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard and correct play. This allows me to control the pot size while seeing how the aggressor reacts, keeping my options open to peel or check-raise depending on the sizing.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Fives) on a board that contains an Ace, a Queen, and a flush draw. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot against the Big Blind. I will bet for value and protection, as my opponent could have a weaker Ace, a Queen, or a diamond draw. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot is appropriate here to build the pot while still being likely to get called by second-best hands.

I have a Jack-high flush draw on an Ace-high board with two diamonds. The opponent's bet is 400 into a 650 pot, giving me favorable pot odds to call (400 to win a total pot of 1450). Calling is a solid defensive play that allows me to realize my equity and potentially hit my flush on the turn or river, while maintaining a manageable pot size given my stack depth.

I have successfully made an Ace-high flush on the turn (Ad, Qd, 5d on board; Jd, 2d in hand). The action on the turn was checked by both players, which typically transitions the game to the river. As the first player to act on the river, and holding the nuts (or a very strong hand), I should lead out with a value bet. A bet of 700 into a pot of 1450 is a solid sizing to get value from potential lower flushes or weaker pairs that might have called the turn.

I have improved to a full house (Fives full of Aces) on the turn. This is an exceptionally strong hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight has led out with a bet of 700 into a pot of 1450 after calling on the flop. This lead often indicates a strong made hand, such as a flush or trips, which my full house dominates. While I am behind to pocket Queens or Ace-Queen, those hands are less likely given the pre-flop action. I will raise for value to build the pot against flushes, trip fives, or even a strong Ace that might not believe I have a boat.

The opponent has shown significant strength with a raise on the turn after my lead. While the board is paired, making a full house a possibility, I have a flush. Folding a flush in this spot would be too tight, and raising is unlikely to be profitable against a range that includes strong sets or better flushes. Calling is the best way to control the pot, see the river, and realize my equity while remaining cautious of potential full houses.

The board is paired with fives, and my opponent showed significant strength on the turn by raising my bet. Although I have an Ace-high flush, the board pair (5h, 5d) makes full houses and quads a real possibility for a range as wide as the Button's. Checking is the safest play, as it allows me to control the pot and potentially induce a bluff from the opponent, while avoiding over-committing against a hand that could already have me beat.

I have a full house, Fives full of Aces (5-5-5-A-A), which is a monster hand. The board is Ad-Qd-5h-5d-Kh. My opponent, Flashlight, called my turn raise and has now checked the river. Given the pre-flop action where Flashlight only called from the Big Blind, it is highly unlikely they hold pocket Aces, Queens, or Kings, which are the only hands (other than the case Five for quads) that could realistically beat me. Flashlight is likely holding a Five for trip fives (which I beat), a strong Ace, or a straight with Jack-Ten. Since the pot is already 5,650 and Flashlight has roughly 6,750 chips remaining, I will place a value bet of 3,000. This amount is large enough to get maximum value from trip fives or a straight, while still being a callable size for a stubborn Ace.

I hold an Ace-high flush (Ad, Qd, Jd, 5d, 2d), which is a very strong hand. While the board is paired with fives, making a full house possible for the opponent, folding a flush here would be overly conservative. The opponent's significant betting on both the turn and the river indicates they have a strong hand, likely a set or a lower flush. Given the pot odds and the strength of my hand, calling is the most profitable action. Raising would likely only be called by a hand that beats me.

K8o is a relatively weak and easily dominated hand, even on the button in a 3-handed game. While it could be used as a steal attempt, the big blind has a massive stack and may defend wide or 3-bet to exert pressure. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve my stack for a more profitable situation.

The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. I have a strong ace-high hand (As-7h).
